content.text: Knowing you have technical debt is not the same as actually resolving it. The gap between "we know about this" and "this is fixed" is a capacity allocation problem. In GenAI teams, this gap is especially costly because unresolved model debt compounds — a prompt that nobody fixed becomes a liability the moment the underlying model is deprecated.
Debt allocation in sprint planning
is the mechanism that converts a debt register from a list of known problems into a managed backlog with predictable resolution velocity.
What Debt Allocation Is

Debt allocation is the deliberate reservation of sprint capacity for technical debt work before the sprint planning meeting starts. Instead of letting debt work compete ad-hoc with feature work for the remaining capacity after features are estimated, the team pre-commits a fraction of their sprint points to debt — and fills that quota from the prioritized debt register.
Why It Matters
AI reliability degrades without debt resolution:

prompt debt and model version debt compound over time. A prompt with hardcoded few-shot examples from 18 months ago produces worse results today than it did when it was written — and will produce worse results next quarter unless someone fixes it.
Debt work never wins in unstructured competition:

when sprint capacity is allocated on a first-come basis, feature requests always win. Product managers bring concrete user value; debt work brings abstract future reliability. Without a pre-committed budget, debt is perpetually deferred.
Model deprecations create forced deadlines:

unlike most software debt, model debt has an external hard deadline when the provider deprecates the version. A team with no debt allocation habit discovers the deprecation date and then scrambles to resolve 20 sprints of model debt in 2 sprints.
The Three Allocation Models

attrs.alt: Three debt allocation models: fixed percentage, debt budget, alternating sprints — plus burn-down chart and PI planning structure

content.text: Model 1: Fixed Percentage (15–20%)

The simplest and most widely used model. Every sprint, 15–20% of total velocity is reserved for debt work before feature stories are planned. A team with 40-point velocity reserves 8 points for debt every sprint, permanently.
Best for: stable teams with a steady flow of debt items, where debt accumulation rate is roughly equal to the debt resolution rate.
Advantage: predictable; easy to communicate to stakeholders ("we always reserve 20% for reliability and maintainability work").
Disadvantage: inflexible. If the team has a critical release coming and 0 open debt items, the reserved capacity is wasted.
Model 2: Debt Budget per Sprint

The team sets a fixed story-point budget for debt work per sprint (e.g., 8 points regardless of total velocity), allocated to the highest-severity open items from the debt register. Unlike the fixed-percentage model, the budget is expressed in absolute points rather than a percentage of velocity — which makes it stable even if velocity fluctuates.
Best for: teams with high debt backlogs where a fixed percentage would allocate too many points, or where debt items vary widely in size.
Advantage: protects against debt taking over the sprint when velocity is high; keeps feature delivery predictable.
Implementation: at sprint planning, pull the top-N debt items from the register by severity score until the budget is exhausted. Carry unused budget to the next sprint (do not lose it).
Model 3: Alternating Sprints

The team alternates between "feature sprints" (100% features, 0% debt) and "hardening sprints" (60% debt, 40% features). This model is appropriate when the debt backlog is large and the team needs concentrated resolution effort, but avoids pure "debt-only sprints" that kill product velocity.
Best for: immediately after a major launch when debt has accumulated rapidly; teams transitioning from a proof-of-concept to a production-quality system.
Advantage: concentrated focus on debt resolution in hardening sprints leads to more complex architectural items getting completed.
Warning: pure debt-only sprints (100% debt, 0% features) destroy team morale and product momentum. Always keep at least 40% feature work in hardening sprints.
Debt Velocity as a Metric

Debt velocity is the number of debt items closed per sprint. Tracking debt velocity separately from feature velocity gives the team and management a clear view of whether debt is being resolved faster than it's being created.

# debt_velocity.py — track debt velocity and print burn-down metrics
import yaml
from datetime import date, datetime
from pathlib import Path
from collections import defaultdict

def compute_debt_velocity(register_path: str = "tech-debt-register.yaml",
                          sprint_length_days: int = 14) -> dict:
    """
    Compute debt velocity: items closed per sprint, grouped by sprint.
    Assumes each item has a 'resolved_date' when status == 'resolved'.
    """
    data = yaml.safe_load(Path(register_path).read_text())
    items = data["debt_items"]
    
    # Find project start date (earliest created_date)
    created_dates = [datetime.strptime(i["created_date"], "%Y-%m-%d").date()
                     for i in items if "created_date" in i]
    if not created_dates:
        return {}
    project_start = min(created_dates)
    
    def sprint_number(d: date) -> int:
        return (d - project_start).days // sprint_length_days + 1
    
    # Count items opened and closed per sprint
    opened_per_sprint = defaultdict(int)
    closed_per_sprint = defaultdict(int)
    for item in items:
        opened = datetime.strptime(item["created_date"], "%Y-%m-%d").date()
        opened_per_sprint[sprint_number(opened)] += 1
        if item.get("status") == "resolved" and "resolved_date" in item:
            resolved = datetime.strptime(item["resolved_date"], "%Y-%m-%d").date()
            closed_per_sprint[sprint_number(resolved)] += 1
    
    max_sprint = max(max(opened_per_sprint), max(closed_per_sprint, default=0))
    
    report = []
    open_count = 0
    for s in range(1, max_sprint + 1):
        open_count += opened_per_sprint[s]
        closed = closed_per_sprint[s]
        open_count -= closed
        report.append({
            "sprint": s,
            "opened": opened_per_sprint[s],
            "closed": closed,
            "net_open": open_count,
        })
    
    total_closed = sum(r["closed"] for r in report)
    velocity = total_closed / max_sprint if max_sprint else 0
    print(f"Debt velocity: {velocity:.1f} items/sprint (last {max_sprint} sprints)")
    print(f"Current open items: {open_count}")
    if velocity > 0:
        print(f"Estimated sprints to clear backlog: {open_count / velocity:.0f}")
    return {"velocity": velocity, "open": open_count, "sprints": report}

result = compute_debt_velocity()
Sprint Planning Integration

Debt work should enter sprint planning through the same process as feature work — with proper story points and acceptance criteria. The only difference is the source: debt items come from the debt register, not the feature backlog.

## Sprint Planning Agenda — Including Debt Allocation

### Pre-Meeting (async, 1 day before)
- Tech lead pulls top debt items from register sorted by severity_score descending
- Annotates each item with estimated effort (if not already scored)
- Identifies which items are "quick wins" (≤ 2 points, score ≥ 10)

### Sprint Planning Meeting

**Step 1: Set capacity (5 min)**
- Total sprint velocity = 42 points (7 engineers × 6 pts/sprint)
- Debt budget = 20% = 8 points (Model 1: Fixed Percentage)
- Feature budget = 34 points

**Step 2: Fill debt quota first (15 min)**
- Pull from register sorted by severity, fit within 8-point budget:
  - DEBT-002 (model pinned version) — 5 points ← critical severity 20
  - DEBT-005 (no retry on tool calls) — 2 points ← severity 12
  - DEBT-009 (log token counts) — 1 point ← severity 8, quick win
  - Total: 8 points ✓
- Each debt item gets written up as a sprint story with acceptance criteria

**Step 3: Fill feature quota (25 min)**
- Standard feature story estimation with remaining 34 points

**Step 4: Verify debt items have acceptance criteria (10 min)**
- DEBT-002: "Model version updated, eval suite runs on new version with ≥ 95% pass rate"
- DEBT-005: "Tool call retries 3× with exponential backoff; circuit breaker opens after 5 failures"
- DEBT-009: "input_tokens, output_tokens logged as structured fields on every LLM span"
PI Planning for GenAI Teams

Program Increment (PI) planning is a quarterly planning ceremony (from SAFe, but applicable more broadly) where the team sets objectives for the next 10–12 weeks. For GenAI teams, technical debt reduction should appear as an explicit PI objective alongside feature delivery objectives.
Architectural-tier debt items belong in PI objectives:
items like "refactor synchronous tool calls to async" or "build multi-model routing layer" are too large for a single sprint story. They become PI objectives with milestones across multiple sprints.
Debt objectives should be measurable:
"improve technical quality" is not a PI objective. "Resolve all model-debt items with severity ≥ 15 by end of PI" is. The debt register provides the measurement.
Include a debt burn-down objective:
"reduce total open debt items from 35 to 20 by end of PI" gives the team and stakeholders a clear target and a chart to watch.
Stakeholder Communication
Product managers and executives resist debt allocation when it is presented as "internal engineering work." The framing matters. Present debt work as a
reliability and quality investment
with concrete user-facing outcomes.

## Example Stakeholder Communication — Debt Allocation

### What we're doing
In Sprint 22, we're reserving 8 of 42 story points (19%) for three technical debt items.

### Why it matters to users
- **DEBT-002** (model upgrade): The current model version is deprecated in 60 days. If we don't
  upgrade now, the AI summarization feature goes offline in Q3. Resolving this now prevents a
  customer-visible outage.

- **DEBT-005** (tool retry logic): Without retries, 3% of agent turns fail when a tool call
  times out. Adding retries will reduce that failure rate to < 0.1%, directly improving the
  success rate metric we track in the graduation dashboard.

- **DEBT-009** (token logging): Without this, we cannot identify which user queries are expensive.
  Adding it unlocks the cost optimization work in the next PI, which has a projected 30% reduction
  in API cost.

### What we're NOT doing
To fit this into the sprint, we're deferring the user-history feature by one sprint (to Sprint 23).
We believe the reliability improvements are higher priority because DEBT-002 has a hard deadline.
Embedding Debt Work into Feature Work
Not all debt resolution requires dedicated sprint stories.
Opportunistic refactoring
is the practice of resolving small debt items while working on adjacent features — the "Boy Scout Rule" applied to AI systems.
When building a new tool integration, add trace correlation IDs to all existing tool calls in the same file (resolves observability debt opportunistically).
When updating the system prompt for a new feature, convert hardcoded few-shot examples to a versioned prompt registry at the same time (resolves prompt debt).

Add a team norm: when any developer touches a file that contains a debt item, they either resolve the debt or add a comment explaining why it wasn't resolved now. This keeps debt visible and not silently accumulated.
Common Anti-Patterns
100% feature sprints:

the team delivers features every sprint but never touches debt. Reliability degrades slowly and invisibly until a model deprecation or a prompt failure causes a customer-visible incident. By then, the debt backlog is so large that resolving it takes 3–4 sprints of concentrated effort.
Pure debt sprints:

100% of sprint capacity is allocated to debt. This happens when a team panics about accumulated debt and overcorrects. Feature delivery stops, product velocity drops to zero, and team morale deteriorates. PM relationships deteriorate when users get zero new functionality for 6 weeks.
Debt allocation without acceptance criteria:
debt stories without clear done criteria get "in progress" indefinitely. Every debt story must have testable completion criteria before sprint planning starts.
Debt budget from which PM can subtract:
if the PM can override debt allocation at planning time by adding a "critical" feature story, the debt budget becomes a fiction. The reserved capacity must be protected by an explicit team agreement.
Best Practices
Decide on an allocation model at the start of the quarter and commit to it for the full PI. Switching models mid-PI makes velocity tracking meaningless.
Track debt velocity on the same dashboard as feature velocity. A team that can show stakeholders both metrics — feature throughput and debt burn-down — has the data to defend its allocation.
Start at 15% allocation even if the debt backlog is large. Build the habit first; increase the percentage in the next PI if the backlog isn't shrinking fast enough.
Treat model deprecation deadlines as hard date constraints in sprint planning. Work backward from the deprecation date and allocate enough capacity to resolve the upgrade before the deadline.
Real-World Example: Planning a Deprecation Sprint

## Model Deprecation Recovery Plan — Sprint Allocation

**Situation:** Model provider announces claude-haiku-3-20240307 deprecated on 2025-06-30
(60 days notice, received 2025-05-01).

**Debt items involved:**
- DEBT-002: Upgrade model version (8 pts) — severity 20 (critical)
- DEBT-007: Build eval suite for new version (13 pts) — severity 15 (prerequisite)
- DEBT-012: Update hardcoded model strings in config (1 pt) — severity 8

**Available sprints:** Sprint 20 (May 5), Sprint 21 (May 19), Sprint 22 (Jun 2),
                      Sprint 23 (Jun 16), Sprint 24 (Jun 30 = deprecation day)

**Allocation plan (overriding fixed-% model for this PI):**
- Sprint 20: DEBT-007 (13 pts) — eval suite build. All other capacity features.
- Sprint 21: DEBT-002 (8 pts) — model upgrade, eval suite run. Features (remaining capacity).
- Sprint 22: Eval results review + fixes. 8pts debt, rest features.
- Sprint 23: DEBT-012 (1pt) + validation + prod deploy of new model. Features (rest).
- Sprint 24: Buffer sprint — no model debt. Full features. Deprecation day safe.

**Communication to PM (2025-05-01):**
We need to allocate ~30% of Sprint 20-22 capacity to the model upgrade. The current
model version is deprecated June 30th. If we miss this deadline, the AI feature goes
offline on July 1st. We will defer [Feature X] to Sprint 25 to fit this plan.
Performance and Scalability

As the team scales, debt accumulation rate scales with it — more engineers shipping more features creates more debt faster. Scale the debt allocation percentage proportionally. A 20-engineer team needs more than 20% allocation if they ship 4× as many features as a 5-engineer team.

For multi-team programs, establish a cross-team debt budget at the PI level. Some architectural debt items (e.g., shared prompt registry, centralized eval framework) are owned by a platform team and funded from a cross-team pool rather than from any single feature team's budget.
Tooling and Ecosystem
Sprint board:
Linear or Jira. Add a "tech-debt" label and filter the board to show the split between debt and feature work in points. This makes the allocation visible to everyone.
Velocity tracking:
track feature velocity and debt velocity as separate series in a Grafana or Tableau chart. The two lines together tell the story of whether the team is keeping pace with its debt accumulation.
PI planning:
Miro for cross-team dependency mapping; the same Linear/Jira board for individual objectives. Export the debt burn-down chart to the PI planning readout for stakeholder visibility.
Comparison with Alternatives
Allocation Approach
Predictability
Flexibility
Best for
Fixed percentage (15–20%)
High
Low
Stable teams, steady debt flow
Debt budget (fixed points)
Medium
Medium
Large backlogs, variable-size items
Alternating sprints
Low
High
Post-launch debt recovery, PoC-to-prod transition
No allocation (ad-hoc)
N/A
High
Not recommended — debt accumulates without bound
Security and Reliability

Security-related debt items (missing input sanitization, unencrypted API keys in config) should bypass the standard debt allocation and go directly into the current sprint as a P0. Do not let security debt wait for a 20% budget slot.

Model deprecation items are reliability emergencies once the deadline is within 60 days. Temporarily increase debt allocation to 30–40% for the sprints needed to complete the upgrade before the deadline.
Mental Model
Think of the debt budget like a
mortgage payment

. You don't make the payment because you have extra money this month — you make it first, before deciding what else to spend on, because not making it has compounding consequences. The fixed-percentage model is the most like a mortgage: the same amount every month, taken off the top before discretionary spending. The debt budget model is more like a savings account: a fixed deposit per month to a dedicated account, allocated on a schedule. Alternating sprints is like paying off a lump sum: intensive for a period, then returning to normal. All three work; the worst option is treating debt as discretionary spending that only happens when there's surplus capacity.
Key Takeaways
Reserve debt capacity before sprint planning starts — not from the leftover points after features are planned. Debt that competes for capacity with features will always lose.

The three allocation models (fixed percentage, debt budget, alternating sprints) suit different situations. Choose one per PI and commit to it; track debt velocity to evaluate whether the chosen model is working.
Debt velocity — items closed per sprint — is the leading indicator of whether the team is reducing its debt backlog or accumulating it faster than it's resolving it.
Architectural debt items (refactoring async tool calls, building multi-model routing) are too large for sprint stories and should be PI objectives with multi-sprint milestones.
Model deprecation deadlines convert model debt from low-urgency to emergency. Plan backward from the deprecation date and temporarily increase allocation to meet the deadline.
Avoid pure debt-only sprints — they destroy team morale and product momentum. Always keep at least 40% feature work even in hardening sprints.
